Antelope||102: WMU 102 sits in the shortgrass and mixed-grass prairie of southeastern Alberta, south and east of Medicine Hat. Classic pronghorn country — flat to gently rolling with coulees cutting through. Vegetation is sparse grazed range and native prairie with sagebrush patches. Visibility is extreme and animals can be spotted at 2+ km on good days.|||Access is via a grid of township and range roads across mostly private agricultural and range land. Crown land is limited — landowner permission is the primary gating factor for hunting here. Build relationships early. Pronghorn tags in this unit are competitive and fill fast; most hunters apply several seasons before drawing. Bring a flat-shooting rifle capable of 300-400m shots. Water sources concentrate animals in dry August and September, which is your best scouting tool. Archery hunters need to be prepared for extreme patience and close-range stalks using terrain features.
Antelope||102, 118: A combined unit covering WMUs 102 and 118 across southeast Alberta mixed-grass prairie east of Lethbridge and north to Brooks. Broad, open country with coulees, wetland sloughs, and occasional tree lines. Good pronghorn numbers in wet years, variable in drought years.|||Township and range road grid provides broad access. Private land is the dominant tenure — landowner relations are essential. This multi-WMU draw gives you access to a large footprint; scout before you go to identify where herds are concentrated. Water and feed are the two things that move antelope in summer heat.
Antelope||104: WMU 104 covers the area southwest of Lethbridge toward the Milk River Ridge. Rolling mixed-grass prairie with badland coulees in the south. One of the stronger pronghorn-density zones in southern AB.|||Highway 4 corridor and range roads provide access. Heavy private land ownership — know your Crown parcels or get permission. The Milk River Ridge area offers broken terrain that holds good bucks. A spotting scope is mandatory kit.
Antelope||104, 106, 112: A large combined zone spanning WMUs 104, 106, and 112 across the heart of southwestern Alberta's pronghorn range. Includes the Milk River drainage, Cardston area, and Lethbridge district. Diverse habitat from open prairie to river coulees and irrigated farmland fringes.|||Broad range road access. Significant private land throughout. This is a big footprint — scout multiple areas and identify current herd concentrations before opening day. Archery hunters should target water sources. Rifle hunters focus on transition terrain between flats and coulees.
Antelope||108: WMU 108 encompasses the Foremost-Bow Island area of southeastern AB — flat to rolling shortgrass prairie with dry coulees and seasonal sloughs. Core pronghorn range with reliable herds in good years. Drought cycles affect numbers noticeably.|||Range road grid access. Heavily private. The southeast corner of Alberta doesn't get as much hunting pressure as some zones, but landowner access remains the key challenge. Scout water sources in late summer — in dry conditions, animals concentrate predictably around permanent water.
Antelope||116: WMU 116 is in the Hanna-Drumheller area, where the Alberta prairie transitions to badlands and the Red Deer River valley. Pronghorn use the open uplands and move into coulees for water and shelter. Not the highest-density pronghorn unit but solid numbers in good years.|||Highway 9 and secondary roads provide access. Mix of private and Crown land — more accessible than pure southeast prairie zones. The badland fringe terrain makes for interesting stalking country. Bucks in this area can be good-quality animals.
Antelope||116, 119, 124, 148: A sprawling multi-WMU zone covering central-eastern AB pronghorn range from Hanna north through the Stettler area. Parkland-prairie transition with aspen bluffs mixed into open range. Pronghorn numbers are lower than the pure southeast prairie but this unit covers a huge area.|||Good road access. Mix of private and Crown. A mobile strategy — covering ground and glassing from high points — works well here. Animals move more than in pure prairie zones.
Antelope||128: WMU 128 sits east of Calgary in the foothills-to-prairie transition around Strathmore and Brooks. Rolling terrain with wetlands, irrigated fields, and native range. A productive zone for antelope that use the crop edges.|||Highway 1 corridor and range roads. Private land is heavy. Antelope here interact with agricultural land more than pure prairie animals — irrigation and crops influence movement patterns. Scout field edges at dawn.
Antelope||128, 140: Combined WMUs 128 and 140 covering central-eastern AB prairie from the Drumheller area east. Open rolling grassland with coulee systems. Productive pronghorn range.|||Range road grid. Significant private land. This is a large combined area that gives hunters flexibility to find concentrations. Glass extensively before committing to a stalk.
Antelope||138: WMU 138 covers the Coronation-Consort area of central-east Alberta — rolling mixed grass prairie with scattered wetlands and aspen bluffs. Transitional zone between pure pronghorn range and the northern edge of their distribution.|||Range road grid access. Good mix of Crown and private. Pronghorn density is lower than the southeast but hunting pressure is also lower. A less-competitive draw.
Antelope||138, 142, 144: Multi-WMU combined zone in central-east AB parkland-prairie. Pronghorn use the grassier portions of these WMUs; presence is patchier than the southeast. A large footprint with lower density.|||Good road access throughout. Scout hard before the season — antelope aren't guaranteed throughout the zone in these transitional WMUs.
Antelope||150: WMU 150 is in the Castor-Coronation area of central Alberta, near the Saskatchewan border. Rolling mixed-grass and fescue prairie with wetland sloughs. Antelope||150, 151, 152: A combined zone near the Saskatchewan border in east-central AB. Parkland and prairie mix — patchy pronghorn habitat. Antelope||160: WMU 160 is in the Oyen area of east-central Alberta. Mixed-grass prairie with wetlands and coulees. Reasonable pronghorn numbers in good years. Near the northern edge of consistent pronghorn range.|||Range road grid. Mix of private and Crown. A good access point to antelope hunting without the extreme private-land concentration of the deep south.
Antelope||160, 162, 163, 164, 166: A large multi-WMU combined zone in eastern Alberta near the Saskatchewan border. Prairie and parkland transition. Elk||102: WMU 102 is in the southern Alberta foothills southwest of Lethbridge, near the Porcupine Hills and Oldman River drainage. Elk||104: WMU 104 covers the Castle River and Waterton area foothills south of Pincher Creek. Elk||108: WMU 108 is in the Pincher Creek-Claresholm area foothills. Rolling to moderate terrain with lodgepole pine, spruce, and open grass parks. Elk||110: WMU 110 spans the Livingstone Range and Crowsnest Pass foothills area. Dramatic terrain — steep ridges, alpine meadows, and heavily timbered valleys. Elk||116: WMU 116 covers the Siksika/Bow River area east of Calgary. A transitional zone between the foothills and parkland. Elk here use river bottoms, aspen bluffs, and agricultural edges. Not classic mountain elk country but solid resident herds.|||Secondary highways and range roads provide broad access. Mix of private and Crown. Elk in this zone are more parkland-style animals — pattern them like deer on food sources and travel corridors. The rut is the best hunting window.
Elk||116, 118, 119: A combined multi-WMU zone covering the Bow River valley and adjacent parkland east of Calgary. Elk use riparian corridors and bush blocks for cover, feeding on agricultural land. Elk||116, 118, 119, 624: Extended multi-WMU combination adding the 624 WMU north of the Trans-Canada. Large prairie-parkland elk range east of Calgary.|||Same access as the 116/118/119 combined zone. Scout for cover and food in this parkland landscape.
Elk||124: WMU 124 is north of Calgary in the Sundre-Caroline area — one of the premier elk WMUs in Alberta. Book camps or plan logistics well in advance.
Elk||124, 128, 142, 144, 148, 150: A large multi-WMU combined zone covering the Sundre to Stettler foothills-to-parkland transition. Focus scouting effort on the western foothills portions for the highest elk density.
Elk||132: WMU 132 is in the Nordegg-Clearwater River country — spectacular Rocky Mountain foothills terrain. Remote lodgepole and spruce country with alpine meadows in the upper reaches. Elk||132, 136, 138: Multi-WMU zone in the Nordegg-Rocky Mountain House foothills. Extensive elk range spanning boreal transition and mountain foothills. Large area with variable elk density.|||Highway 11 and 22 corridors with forestry road access. Focus on drainages and cutblocks in the western portions for highest elk concentration.
Elk||151: WMU 151 is in the Battle River area of central-east AB. Parkland elk hunting — aspen bluffs, agricultural edges, and willow draws. A more accessible elk draw than the mountain WMUs. Elk here are patternable parkland animals. Rut hunting in November is prime.
Elk||151, 152: Combined WMUs 151 and 152 in east-central AB parkland. Elk use bush corridors between agricultural land. A large footprint in accessible terrain.|||Good road access. Private land dominant — build landowner relationships before hunting season.
Elk||156: WMU 156 is in the Provost area near the Saskatchewan border. Mixed-grass and parkland elk habitat. Elk herds here are resident agricultural edge animals. Less glamorous than mountain draws but the effort-to-reward ratio can be excellent for hunters with landowner access.
Elk||156, 158, 160: Combined zone in east-central AB near the Saskatchewan border. Parkland elk across a broad footprint.|||Range road access throughout. Scout for cover and agricultural edges.
Elk||162: WMU 162 covers the Lloydminster-area parkland near the Saskatchewan border. Rolling aspen parkland with agricultural clearings. Elk are present but in lower density than western WMUs.|||Good secondary highway and range road access. Significant private land. Less competitive draw — a good option for hunters building their elk points bank.
Elk||162, 163, 164, 166: Multi-WMU zone in northeastern Alberta parkland near the Saskatchewan border. Large combined footprint with variable elk density across the parkland mosaic.|||Good road access. Scout before the season to locate current herds.
Elk||200: WMU 200 is in the Drayton Valley-Brazeau River country west of Edmonton — boreal and foothills transition. Good elk country with a mix of lodgepole pine, aspen, and spruce. Elk||200, 202, 203, 232, 234: Large multi-WMU zone in the Drayton Valley-Edson boreal foothills. Extensive elk range across multiple drainages. Elk||204: WMU 204 is in the Edson-Hinton area foothills — excellent elk country at the base of the Rockies. Lodgepole and spruce timber with open benches and meadows. September bugling here can be tremendous. Plan for crowds on main forestry roads during early season.
Elk||204 ,228, 230: Multi-WMU zone in the Edson-Hinton boreal foothills. Same high-quality habitat as WMU 204 across a larger footprint.|||Forestry road network. 4x4 recommended. Large area provides opportunities to get away from pressure.
Elk||204, 228, 230: Multi-WMU zone in the Edson-Hinton boreal foothills. Same high-quality habitat as WMU 204 across a larger footprint.|||Forestry road network. 4x4 recommended. Large area provides opportunities to get away from pressure.
Elk||206: WMU 206 spans the Whitecourt-Swan Hills area — a major elk zone in central Alberta. Boreal mixedwood with extensive cutblocks from forestry operations. High elk density in good years.|||Highway 43 and 32 corridors with an extensive logging road network. 4x4 essential. Whitecourt-area elk are well-known among central AB hunters. Elk||206, 222, 226, 244, 246: Large multi-WMU boreal zone in the Whitecourt-Fox Creek area. Extensive elk range across the boreal mixed wood.|||Logging road networks. 4x4 essential. Mobile strategy — cover ground to find active cutblocks.
Elk||208: WMU 208 is in the Barrhead-Westlock area northwest of Edmonton. Boreal parkland transition with aspen-dominated mixedwood. Elk numbers are present but this WMU is more parkland than mountain — a different style of hunt.|||Secondary highways and range roads. Good access generally. Mix of private and Crown land.
Elk||208, 210, 220: Multi-WMU zone in the Barrhead-Westlock-Slave Lake area boreal parkland. Large footprint covering the boreal edge northwest of Edmonton.|||Good road access. Scout for cover concentrations in this predominantly agricultural-fringe landscape.
Elk||212: WMU 212 is an antlerless-only zone near the Athabasca River in north-central Alberta. Boreal forest with spruce, aspen, and muskeg. An elk population management draw.|||Forestry roads and secondary highways. 4x4 for back-country access. Antlerless hunts here are specifically to manage cow numbers — a population tool rather than a trophy hunt.
Elk||214: WMU 214 is north of Edmonton near Athabasca. Boreal parkland with aspen and mixed forest. Elk use agricultural edges and forest openings. An accessible boreal elk draw.|||Good highway and secondary road access. Mix of private agricultural land and Crown forest.
Elk||214, 314: Combined WMUs 214 and 314 north of Edmonton and into boreal country near Lac La Biche. Broader access footprint across boreal parkland.|||Good highway corridors with secondary road access.
Elk||216: WMU 216 covers the Lac La Biche-Smoky Lake area boreal parkland. Aspen and mixedwood with agricultural clearings. Elk use the bush corridors extensively.|||Highway 36 and secondary roads. Mix of agricultural private and Crown forest land.
Elk||216, 320: Combined zone spanning the Lac La Biche area boreal parkland into the boreal proper north of Athabasca. Large footprint with variable elk density.|||Good road access. Elk density higher in the western portions of this combined zone.
Elk||221: WMU 221 is in the Vermilion-Wainwright area of east-central Alberta. Parkland elk hunting — aspen bluffs and agricultural edges. Lower elk density than western zones but accessible hunting for eastern Alberta residents.|||Good range road and highway access. Significant private land.
Elk||221, 322: Multi-WMU zone in east-central parkland. Large combined footprint.|||Good road access. Scout for current herd locations — distribution is variable.
Elk||224: WMU 224 is near Two Hills-Vegreville in the aspen parkland east of Edmonton. Agricultural-edge elk hunting. Elk numbers have been building in the parkland over recent decades.|||Range roads and secondary highways. Significant private land. Elk here respond to calls during the rut. Tree stand hunting on agricultural edges is also productive.
Elk||236: WMU 236 is in the Mayerthorpe-Sangudo area west of Edmonton. Boreal parkland with agricultural clearings and mixedwood forest. A productive elk zone for the central boreal transition.|||Highway 43 corridor and secondary roads. Good access. Mix of Crown and private. Elk bugling in September can be heard right from main roads in good years.
Elk||236, 238, 256: Multi-WMU zone in the Mayerthorpe-Sangudo boreal area. Expanded footprint for mobile hunting.|||Good road access. Focus on cutblocks and forest edges.
Elk||240: WMU 240 is north of Edmonton near Westlock in the boreal parkland. Aspen parkland with agricultural clearing. A lower-pressure elk zone compared to foothills WMUs.|||Secondary highways and range roads. Good access. Mix of private and Crown.
Elk||240, 242: Combined parkland zone northwest of Edmonton. Accessible boreal-transition elk hunting across a broad footprint.|||Good road access. Agricultural-edge elk hunting strategy applies.
Elk||250: WMU 250 is in the Peace River country around Fairview-Grimshaw. Boreal parkland elk habitat at the northern edge of consistent elk range in Alberta.|||Highway 2 north and secondary roads. Good access. Elk density is lower here — this is a less competitive draw for hunters in the Peace Country.
Elk||252: WMU 252 covers the Grande Prairie area boreal parkland and foothills transition. A productive elk zone combining agricultural edge hunting with foothills access. Grande Prairie is underrated elk country.|||Good highway access — Highway 40 and 43 corridors. Forestry roads for backcountry. Mix of private agricultural and Crown land. Elk can be numerous here; the area doesn't get the pressure of more famous southern WMUs.
Elk||252, 254, 258, 260: Large multi-WMU zone in the Grande Prairie and north Peace Country. Extensive elk range across the boreal foothills.|||Highway 40 and forestry road networks. 4x4 for backcountry. Large footprint — scout for current concentrations.
Elk||300: WMU 300 is a special management zone near Elk Island National Park east of Edmonton. This is a highly managed urban-fringe elk population with periodic draws to manage numbers. The terrain is flat boreal aspen park.|||Limited access — managed Crown land and parkland fringe. This draw is specifically a population management tool. Elk||300 (Area A): WMU 300 Area A is the northern sub-zone of the Elk Island management area east of Edmonton. Flat boreal aspen parkland. A managed population draw.|||Restricted access per draw conditions. Coordinate with Fish and Wildlife on access details.
Elk||300 (Area B): WMU 300 Area B is the southern sub-zone of the Elk Island management area. Same flat aspen parkland as Area A. A managed population draw.|||Restricted access per draw conditions. Coordinate with Fish and Wildlife on access details.
Elk||302: WMU 302 is in the Edson area foothills east of Hinton. Boreal lodgepole and spruce with cutblocks. Good elk habitat in a less-pressured zone than WMU 204.|||Highway 16 corridor and logging roads. 4x4 essential for back roads.
Elk||303: WMU 303 is in the Edson-Hinton corridor boreal foothills. Lodgepole and spruce terrain with open benches. Consistent elk numbers.|||Logging road network off Highway 16. 4x4 recommended.
Elk||304: WMU 304 covers the Cadomin-Luscar area south of Hinton. Mountain foothills with open subalpine meadows in the upper elevations. Elk||305: WMU 305 is in the Grande Cache area — stunning Rocky Mountain foothills terrain. Rugged ridges, deep valleys, and classic elk country. Expect challenging terrain — Grande Cache area hunts are rewarding but not easy.
Elk||306: WMU 306 is south of Grande Cache in the upper Smoky River country. Remote foothills terrain. One of the wilder elk zones in Alberta.|||Highway 40 south of Grande Cache then forestry roads. 4x4 essential. Horses are a significant advantage in the backcountry sections. Expect lower pressure in exchange for more difficult access.
Elk||308: WMU 308 covers the Kakwa River area in the northern foothills west of Grande Prairie. The Kakwa area is genuinely remote — pack accordingly. A multi-day camp hunt is the right approach.
Elk||310: WMU 310 is in the Valleyview area boreal parkland. Aspen-dominated mixedwood with agricultural clearings. A parkland-style elk hunt north of the foothills.|||Highway 34 corridor and secondary roads. Good access. Elk use the bush corridors between farms. Rut calling works well in open parkland.
Elk||312: WMU 312 covers the Swan Hills area — classic boreal elk country with dense mixedwood and cutblocks. High elk density in a timber-harvesting landscape. Elk||318: WMU 318 is in the Lesser Slave Lake area boreal parkland. Mix of agricultural and forest land around the lake. Elk use the forest and agricultural edge.|||Highway 2 and secondary roads north. Good access. Mix of private and Crown.
Elk||324: WMU 324 is in the Manning-Peace River area boreal parkland. Northern AB elk habitat — aspen and mixedwood with agricultural clearings at the northern edge of consistent elk range.|||Highway 35 corridor and secondary roads. Good access for the north. Less competitive draw than southern mountain WMUs.
Elk||330: WMU 330 is in the Slave Lake area boreal forest. Dense mixedwood and spruce with muskeg pockets. Elk numbers present but distributed through dense timber.|||Secondary roads and forestry access routes. High-clearance vehicle essential. Elk hunting in dense boreal requires still-hunting or patient stand hunting on trails.
Elk||332: WMU 332 covers the High Prairie area boreal parkland. Mix of agricultural land and mixedwood forest. Elk use the transition zones between farmland and bush.|||Highway 2 north corridor and secondary roads. Good access. Private and Crown mix.
Elk||334: WMU 334 is in the McLennan-Falher area of northwestern Alberta. Boreal parkland with agricultural influence. Elk are present along the Peace River country fringe.|||Highway 2 and secondary roads. Good access. A less pressured draw in the north Peace Country.
Elk||336: WMU 336 covers the Peace River town area boreal parkland. Agricultural edges and mixedwood forest along the Peace River valley. Elk use the valley and adjacent bush.|||Highway 2 through Peace River and secondary roads. Good access. The Peace River valley terrain is unique — deep coulees and bench country above the river with heavy bush.
Elk||337: WMU 337 is northwest of Peace River in boreal parkland. Agricultural and forest transition. Elk are present at lower density.|||Secondary roads north and west of Peace River. Good access. A less competitive draw for northern hunters.
Elk||338: WMU 338 covers the Grimshaw-Manning corridor. Boreal parkland with agricultural influence. Elk at the northern fringe of their range.|||Highway 35 corridor. Good access. Elk density lower here — a draw for hunters in the area.
Elk||340: WMU 340 is in the High Level area — near the northern limit of elk range in Alberta. Boreal forest and agricultural land near the Manning-High Level highway corridor.|||Highway 35 north. Good road access. Elk are present but at low density. A draw for local hunters.
Elk||342: WMU 342 covers the Rainbow Lake area in far northwest Alberta. Transitional boreal forest. Elk present at low density near the northern edge of range.|||Highway 58 and secondary roads. Remote but accessible via highway. A less competitive north-country draw.

Elk||346: WMU 346 covers the Fort Vermilion area. North Peace boreal parkland. Elk are present in the agricultural clearing and forest transition around Fort Vermilion.|||Highway 697 and secondary roads. Remote north. A far-north elk draw.

Elk||412: WMU 412 is in the McLeod River drainage east of Hinton. Boreal foothills with lodgepole and cutblocks from timber operations. Elk||416: WMU 416 is in the Obed-Peers area between Edson and Hinton. Foothills terrain with excellent elk habitat. A productive and relatively accessible draw.|||Highway 16 and forestry roads. 4x4 recommended. A solid mid-mountain elk WMU.
Elk||417: WMU 417 covers the Lobstick River area south of Hinton. Foothills lodgepole and spruce with excellent elk habitat. Less pressure than WMU 404 area.|||Forestry Trunk Road access. 4x4 essential. Good option for quality elk country with slightly more manageable competition.
Elk||418: WMU 418 is in the Berland River area northwest of Grande Cache. Remote foothills terrain. Elk||420: WMU 420 covers the upper Smoky River tributaries northwest of Grande Cache. Some of the most spectacular and remote foothills terrain in Alberta. Exceptional elk country.|||Forestry roads north of Highway 40 in the Grande Cache area. 4x4 essential, horses highly recommended for backcountry. This is a destination draw — hunters travel from across Canada for tags in this region.
Elk||422: WMU 422 is in the Wapiti River area west of Grande Prairie. Foothills terrain mixing open benches with dense valley spruce. Good elk numbers.|||Highway 40 south of Grande Prairie then forestry roads. 4x4 essential.
Elk||426: WMU 426 covers the upper Kakwa River area in the northern foothills. Remote terrain with good elk numbers and low pressure.|||Forestry roads west of Grande Prairie. 4x4 essential. A remote draw — multi-day camp hunt required.
Elk||428: WMU 428 is in the Narraway River drainage in the northern foothills near the BC border. Very remote elk country at the northern limit of Rocky Mountain foothills. Plan accordingly — this is not a day-hunt situation.
Elk||430: WMU 430 covers the upper Wapiti River area west of Grande Prairie. Good foothills elk country with a mix of accessible and remote terrain.|||Forestry roads off Highway 43. 4x4 essential.
Elk||432: WMU 432 is in the Simonette River area. Foothills and boreal transition. Elk present in good numbers in the western foothills portions.|||Forestry roads. 4x4 recommended. A productive northern foothills elk draw.
Elk||434: WMU 434 covers the Little Smoky River area. Boreal foothills with lodgepole and extensive cutblocks from timber operations. Good elk habitat.|||Highway 43 corridor and logging roads. 4x4 essential. Active logging in areas — work the fresh cutblocks.
Elk||438: WMU 438 is antlerless elk in the Hinton area foothills. Same high-quality mountain foothills terrain as adjacent antlered elk WMUs. This is a population management draw.|||Same access as the Hinton-area WMUs — Highway 40 and forestry roads. 4x4 recommended.
Elk||439: WMU 439 is antlerless elk in the Grande Cache area foothills. Management draw in excellent foothills terrain. Same country as the antlered draws in this region.|||Highway 40 through Grande Cache and forestry roads. 4x4 essential.
Elk||441: WMU 441 antlerless elk near the Hinton-Edson area. Boreal foothills management draw.|||Forestry road access. 4x4 recommended.
Elk||444: WMU 444 antlerless elk in the Grande Prairie foothills area. Management draw in northern foothills terrain.|||Forestry roads west of Grande Prairie. 4x4 recommended.
Elk||500: WMU 500 is in the Peace River country north of Grande Prairie. Boreal parkland with agricultural influence. Elk are present but at the northern edge of consistent range.|||Secondary highways and range roads. Good access. Northern Peace Country elk draw.

Elk||502: WMU 502 is in the Spirit River area. Peace Country boreal parkland. Elk present in agricultural-forest transition.|||Highway 49 corridor. Good road access. Mix of private and Crown.
Elk||503: WMU 503 covers the Beaverlodge-Grande Prairie area. Agricultural parkland edge. Elk are resident animals in the bush corridors.|||Good highway and range road access. Significant private land — permission key.

Elk||505: WMU 505 covers the Pouce Coupe area near the BC border. Transitional boreal parkland. Elk present.|||Near BC border on Highway 2. Good access.
Elk||506: WMU 506 is in the Fairview-Grimshaw area boreal parkland. Northern Peace Country elk at the edge of their range.|||Highway 2 north. Good access. Lower density elk draw.
Elk||507: WMU 507 covers the Manning-Hotchkiss area. Far north boreal parkland. Elk present at low density at the northern fringe.|||Highway 35 corridor. Good access for the north.
Elk||508: WMU 508 is in the Peace River-North Peace area. Boreal forest. Elk present in the forest fringe.|||Highway 2 north. Good access.
Elk||509: WMU 509 covers the Wanham-Valleyview area boreal parkland. Mix of forest and agricultural land. Elk use the transition zones.|||Secondary roads in the Valleyview area. Good access.

Elk||537: WMU 537 is in the Saddle Hills area north of Grande Prairie. Boreal parkland with agricultural influence. Elk present.|||Secondary roads north of Grande Prairie. Good access.
Elk||544: WMU 544 covers the Peace Country boreal. Elk present in the forest fringe.|||Secondary roads. Good access for Peace Country area.

Goat||Area I: Area I encompasses mountain goat habitat in the southern Alberta Rockies, including portions of the Livingstone Range and Highwood area near WMUs 104-110. Terrain is extreme — vertical cliff faces, talus slopes, and exposed rocky ridges at 2,000-3,000m elevation. Goats live in some of the most vertical terrain accessible to hunters in Canada.|||Access via Forestry Trunk Road and backcountry trails in the southern Rockies. Horses or multi-day backpack trips required. Goat tags in Alberta are among the most coveted draws in the province — odds are in low single digits and most hunters apply for 10+ years before drawing. Physical fitness and comfort in exposed mountain terrain are non-negotiable. Pack-out of a goat is notoriously challenging — nanny goats die on cliff edges and ledges. Plan every aspect of the pack-out before the shot. This is a once-in-a-lifetime hunt for most Alberta hunters.
Goat||Area R: Area R covers mountain goat habitat in the central Alberta Rockies, including portions of the Brazeau-Clearwater drainage area near WMUs 404-408. Rugged alpine and subalpine terrain — cliff bands, moraines, and open rocky slides. Goat populations are carefully managed.|||Multi-day backcountry expedition required. Horses strongly recommended due to pack-out weight and terrain difficulty. The central Rockies area is spectacular but demanding. This tag is extremely rare. Know how to navigate steep alpine terrain safely and have practiced long-range shooting — goats often present at awkward angles on cliff faces. A guide or experienced partner is strongly recommended.
Goat||Area U: Area U covers mountain goat habitat in the northern Alberta Rockies, including portions of the Grande Cache-Kakwa area near WMUs 305-420. Remote northern foothills mountains with dramatic cliff and alpine terrain. The least-pressured of the three goat areas.|||Extremely remote — helicopter or extended pack-horse expeditions are the norm for the most productive areas. Highway 40 through Grande Cache provides the closest road access. A goat tag in Area U is a serious logistical undertaking. Northern location means earlier winter conditions — plan for snow at elevation in late September. A guide is strongly recommended.
Moose||102: WMU 102 is in the southern Alberta foothills — marginal but real moose habitat. Moose in the 100-series WMUs use willow-choked creek drainages, beaver pond complexes, and the upper-elevation spruce. Moose in the foothills can be called during the rut in late September-October. Work willow flats and beaver ponds at dawn and dusk. Drawing a moose tag in the foothills is a quality opportunity — pressure is lower than boreal WMUs and the terrain is spectacular.

Moose||200: WMU 200 is in the central Alberta boreal parkland and forest transition — productive moose habitat with a mix of aspen, spruce, and wetland complexes. This is the heart of the Alberta parkland moose range. Moose densities in the 200-series are generally good.|||Access via secondary highways and forestry/logging roads. 4x4 recommended for back roads. A canoe or small boat can dramatically expand your access to productive beaver pond and wetland habitat. A cooler, game cart, and ideally an ATV should be in your plan.

Moose densities in the 200-series are generally good.|||Access via secondary highways and forestry/logging roads. 4x4 recommended for back roads. A canoe or small boat can dramatically expand your access to productive beaver pond and wetland habitat. Rut calling in late September to mid-October is the premier hunting window — bulls are vocal and responsive. Pack-out planning is critical with moose; a full bull is 400-600 lbs of meat. A cooler, game cart, and ideally an ATV should be in your plan.

Moose||300: WMU 300 is in the north-central Alberta boreal forest — classic moose habitat. Dense spruce, aspen, and black spruce bogs with beaver ponds and willow flats throughout. Moose densities in the 300-series can be very high in productive areas.|||Access via logging and forestry roads off main highway corridors. A high-clearance 4x4 or ATV is essential to reach quality habitat off the main roads. The 300-series represents some of the best calling moose country in Alberta — bulls respond aggressively during the mid-October rut. Bug season is severe in early September; plan accordingly. Pack-out infrastructure (ATV, game bags, quarters) is mandatory planning for any moose hunt in dense boreal.

Moose||500: WMU 500 is in the Peace River country or boreal north of Alberta — among the best moose habitat in the province. Moose populations in the northern 500-series WMUs can be outstanding.|||Access via logging roads, seismic lines, and highway secondary networks. 4x4 or ATV mandatory — many access roads are soft and wet. Bug pressure in early season (September) can be severe — plan for it. Pack-out of a northern moose is a serious logistical exercise; many hunters use ATVs or skinboats/canoes. A multi-day camp in the 500-series is the right approach.

Mule Deer||102: WMU 102 is in the southern Alberta foothills — classic mule deer country. Rolling grass ridges, coulees, and river breaks mix with lodgepole pine pockets in the western portions. Visibility is good for glassing. Trophy potential is high in southern AB mule deer; genetics are excellent in this region.|||Highway 22 (Cowboy Trail) and range road networks provide access. Moderate private land — some Crown is available. Spot-and-stalk is the dominant tactic. Glass from high points and work downwind. Quality bucks in this area regularly score in trophy class.

Moderate private land — some Crown is available. Spot-and-stalk is the dominant tactic. Glass from high points and work downwind. The November rut is the premier window — bucks throw caution to the wind chasing does across open terrain. A flat-shooting rifle and quality optics are essential. Quality bucks in this area regularly score in trophy class.

Wild Turkey||300: WMU 300 wild turkey near Elk Island National Park east of Edmonton. Alberta's wild turkey population is a relatively recent established breeding population in the parkland east of Edmonton. Terrain is flat aspen parkland and agricultural fields.|||Access via secondary roads east of Edmonton near the Elk Island area. Turkey in Alberta respond to calling in spring (if a spring draw exists) or roost-based hunting in fall. Scouting food sources — grain fields, berries, acorns — is the most productive pre-season activity. Alberta turkeys are not as naive as newly stocked birds; hunting pressure has made them increasingly wary.
Wild Turkey||999: WMU 999 is the province-wide umbrella designation for wild turkey draws not tied to a specific WMU. Alberta's turkey population is primarily established in the parkland zone east and south of Edmonton, along the Bow River corridor, and in select river valley habitats.|||Access varies by specific hunting area. Scout your intended hunting area well before the season — turkey distribution is not uniform and finding active flocks is the first challenge. Calling is the most exciting and effective tactic. Set up near established roosts or travel corridors between roost trees and food sources.
